source.title:
The Man with
                                the Secret of Happiness

Erwerb: Schenkung der Erben Stefan Zweigs

additional.context:
## Dieses Dokument

- Titel: The Man with
                                the Secret of Happiness
- Signatur: SZ-AP2/W-H17.3
- Sprache: Englisch
- Objekttyp: Typoskriptdurchschlag
- Umfang: 9 Blatt,
                                                  korrigiert
- Hand: Lotte Zweig
- Anmerkungen: Korrekturen von Lotte Zweigs Hand

pages.text:
Digest, New York
1939
THE MAN WITH THE SECRET OF HAPPINESS
by Stefan Zweig, 49 Hallam Street
London W1

I should be ungrateful indeed had I forgotten the
person who showed me two of the most difficult things on
earth: how, by means of {an inner freedom,} the ~~sovereignty of the mind,~~ a man
can free himself from the strongest power in this world,
the power of money; and how a man can live among his fellow
human beings without making a single enemy. I have honestly
tried to learn from him this rare art, but I must confess
that hitherto I have succeeded neither in the one respect
nor the other.

It was in a very simple way that I came to know this
{unique} ~~singular~~ individual in the little town where I was living
at the time. One afternoon I was taking my faithful
spaniel for a walk. Kaspar, who as a rule frisked along
ahead of me, on this occasion behaved in an extremely foolish
manner. He lay on his back and rolled frenziedly on the
ground, all four feet in the air, rubbed himself against
every tree, whimpered, ~~[...4...]~~ and growled incessantly. But
I took this to be spring-time naughtiness and paid no further
heed to it.

All of a sudden I became aware that someone was walking
by my side. Looking up, I saw that a man of about thirty,
poorly dressed, collarless and hatless, was keeping pace
with me. A beggar, I thought involuntarily, and was about
to put my hand in my pocket. But the stranger smiled
tranquilly at me out of clear starry blue eyes as though we
were old friends. "He's got a tick, poor chap," he said,
pointing to my dog. "I could tell that at once. Come
along, we'll go over to that bench in the park, and I'll
have it out in no time."
I must remark at this point that {in addressing me he used „Du“, the} he ~~addressed me in the~~
second person singular, which in German is only employed
among people ~~of the same class who have been~~ {who are} on intimate
pages.notes: Die Seite enthält handschriftliche Korrekturen und Einfügungen. Der Text ist teilweise durchgestrichen.

pages.text:
--2--
terms {for years;} if, therefore, I had attached great
importance to the ordinary conventions I should inevitably
have sent this stranger who {had} ~~too familiar~~ addressed me thus familiarly
about his business. But there was such warm friendliness
in the ~~familiarity of~~ his gaze that in some almost indefinable
way his suggestion assumed for me the nature of a command.
Without a moment's hesitation I crossed the street with him
and we sat down side by side on the bench. He called the
dog {to him} with a shrill whistle.

And strange to say, my Kaspar, who usually sniffed and
snuffled round every stranger and made a great to-do before
letting himself be touched, responded as obediently as I
had {myself} ~~just done~~ and at a sign from the {man} ~~stranger~~ placed his
head with its heavy ears on his knee. {The unknown man}
He searched about in {the dog's} his coat for a time with long, horny
fingers, {the nails of which} ~~the nails~~ shone with remarkable brightness.
Then, with a satisfied "Aha!" he gripped the patient animal
between his knees and began a lengthy operation. It must
have been painful, for Kaspar whimpered faintly several
times, but stood still on tautened, trembling legs, and made
no effort to wriggle free.

Suddenly the stranger released him from the vice-like
grip of his knees. "Here it is," he laughed, triumphantly
holding something in the air. "Now run along, doggie,"
he said to the still trembling animal, who understood {him}
at once and whirled off like a top. Then the stranger
rose, and with a nod and a "Grüss Gott," walked coolly on
with the sure step of a man who has made a good job of
something.

I was so taken aback by his sudden departure that it
only later occurred to me that I really ought to have given
the {poor} fellow, who ~~was quite obviously wretchedly poor,~~ something
for his trouble - some money or a few cigarettes. At any
rate I ought to at least have thanked him. But there was
pages.notes: Seite 2 des Textes (paginiert als --2--). Enthält zahlreiche Korrekturen.

pages.text:
--3--
the same finality and self-possession about his going as
his coming. Still pondering on the {man's} odd behaviour of this
~~stranger,~~ I walked home, and {as} since Kaspar was by far the
most important personage in our household, I could not
refrain from reporting his adventure. Whereupon our old
cook remarked with eager matter-of-factness: "Oh, that
was Anton. He's got an eye for everything."

It was thus I learned that the good Samaritan was
called Anton, and I went on to ask the cook what was his
trade and what he actually did. "Nothing," said our cook
peevishly, as though she regarded my question as a personal
insult, and then, answering, as women will, with a question,
added: "What does he want with a trade?"

Anxious not to provoke her, I merely remarked {very}
tentatively that everyone had to have something to live on.
"Oh, he doesn't need anything," she said proudly. "Everyone
is only too glad to give him whatever he wants." {I admitted}
{I was} ~~I was~~ ~~obliged~~ shame-facedly to confess that from me at least
this {queer} ~~stranger~~ Anton had asked for nothing. But this only
fortified our cook in her pride. "Yes, he never asks
anyone for anything. He doesn't care a straw about money,
he doesn't need it."

Well, this was odd. Hitherto I had always believed
that everyone needed money in order to live, and that
{those} ~~anyone~~ who had none must earn a living by doing some kind
of work. I knew that in our little town, as in every other
town in the world, every crust of bread and every glass of
beer, a lodging for the night and a coat to wear, had to be
paid for in cash, by the rich sometimes at the end of the
week or the month, by the poor on the spot; that here as
everywhere no work of any kind was done for nothing, indeed
that all work was performed for a fixed trade union rate of
pay.

How came this spare little fellow, then, with the

--4--
threadbare trousers, to get round this law? How was it
that he eluded the finely-meshed net of agreements and rates
which to-day is spread over the whole of our world, and was
utterly carefree and happy? I resolved to investigate his
special technique and was ~~soon~~ obliged to admit that
our cook had been right: this fellow Anton had no kind of
settled job, he really did wander about all day long.
Every morning he set out and strolled through the town, with
the shrewdest, most watchful pair of eyes, to be sure, a
man could possibly have in his head. He observed everything
and noticed {all shortcomings} ~~everything~~. He would stop the driver of a cart
and draw his attention to the fact that his horse was {imperfectly} ~~badly~~
harnessed. The man would laugh and attend to the matter,
{for Anton had been quite correct.} Then Anton would walk
past a fence, beckon to the owner of the garden and advise
him to have the rotting wood {painted,} ~~painted,~~ and the latter
would {ask him to do} ~~entrust him with~~ the job. Everyone let him do whatever
he suggested, because everyone knew that Anton never intruded
out of cupidity, but merely disinterestedly and sincerely
drew people's attention to {things} ~~anything~~ that was out of order.
How many jobs have I not seen him putting his hand to!

Once I found him sitting in a shoemaker's mending shoes,
{extra} once acting as an additional waiter at a party, once taking
some children out for a walk, another time selling apples
among the market-women; the owner of one of the stalls was
in child-bed, and so - everyone turned to him {in an emergency}
~~occasiona~~ - she had got him to take her place for a few days.

All the same, there are plenty of such handy fellows
who can turn a hand to anything, and are always ready to
help. The special and unique thing about Anton, however,
was that he firmly refused to take more money, even for many
hours of hard work, than precisely {what} the amount he needed for
{that} ~~one day;~~ {went well} and when things {were going well with him} he flatly
declined to accept any payment whatever. "I'll come to you

--5--
later on if I need anything," he would say in such cases.
And I soon became aware that this gaunt, {usually} ill-shaven
ragged fellow had discovered for himself a new and
fundamentally anti-capitalist system; he had faith in the
decency of human beings. Instead of depositing money in
a saving's bank, he preferred to accumulate {easily realisable}
capital in the form of moral obligations with almost all the
people in the little town; he invested his little {fortune} in
invisible credits, for even the most obstinate and mis-
trustful could not escape a certain feeling of indebtedness
{to} towards a person who did not make a business of his work and
his capacities, but did anything that was asked of him {as}
though it were {the most natural thing in the world,} ~~by way of favour,~~ without
{without thought of fixed compensation.} demanding immediate payment. (One only had to gaze after
Anton in the street to realise in what special esteem people
held him for this; everyone had a cordial greeting for
him, everyone shook him by the hand, and never have I
understood so well the power wielded by a man who has the
great secret of life in his blood, the secret of taking no
thought for the morrow, and of genuinely, as the school-books
say, trusting in God. {This "Do-nothing" and yet "Do-all", this simple}
carefree man in the shabby coat, walked through the town
like a landowner inspecting his estates, with a genial and
friendly air; he could enter every door, sit down at every
table, everything was his to command, because he never wanted
anything but the barest necessities. Soon I came to under-
stand the secret of his power over people - he served not for
money but out of sheer human kindness, and it was on that
account that everyone respected him.

I must frankly admit that {notice} ~~it gradually began to annoy me to~~ {that} he passed me
of all people merely with a casual greeting as though I were

--6--
more or less a stranger. Evidently his delicacy of feeling
{led} him to assume that I, as a writer, could
not possibly require his services; and I felt myself in
some way or other excluded by this polite indifference from
a large and friendly community. And so the {next} time
something was out of order in our house - water was dripping
from a gutter - I seized the opportunity to suggest to my
cook that Anton should be asked to have a look at it;
perhaps he might be able to discover what had gone wrong;
she was to write to him or send for him.

"You can't send for him, he is always staying with
someone different" - she replied. "And you can't write to him, either. But
I'll get word to him somehow." Once more I had learned
something fresh about this strange individual: a veritable
bird of passage, he had no home, no permanent address. And
yet no one was easier to get in touch with than he; a sort
of wireless telephony connected him with the whole town. It
was sufficient to tell the first person you met in the
street, "I want Anton," and he would tell the next person he
met, and so it would go on until someone ran across him. And
indeed that very afternoon he turned up. He looked at
everything in the house with his shrewd, bright eyes, pointing
out, as he walked through the garden, that here a bush wanted
{trimming,} there a young tree transplanting. We took
him up to the roof, where he inspected the gutter. "This is
where the trouble is," he announced after a few minutes, and
set to work forthwith.

It was a pleasure to see how skilfully he tackled the job,
but unfortunately I had no time to watch him for very long;
I was expecting some friends from France, and after two hours
in their company I had forgotten all about the good Anton.
Then there was a knock at the door and he entered the room
in his shirt-sleeves, not in the least abashed at the sight

--7--
of my visitors. "Don't bother, I shan't disturb
you," he said in his genial, self-assured way, smiling
good-naturedly at the well-dressed strangers. "I
only wanted to tell you everything's all right now. You
go and have a look later on. Grüss Gott."

Again he had {left} without ~~thanking him.~~ But this time at least I rushed off to tell
the cook to pay him well, for I myself - so strongly was one
conscious of this man's complete independence of money -
{not} had the courage to offer him anything. {Then} I return to my visitors. But
in a few minutes I began to wonder uneasily whether the cook
had paid him handsomely enough. I sent for her and asked her
if he had been satisfied. "Well, of course," she said.
"He's always satisfied. I wanted to give him six Schillings,
but he would only take two. That would see him through for
to-day and to-morrow, he said. But if the Herr Doktor, he
said, ever had an old winter coat to spare, that would be
very nice."

I find it hard to describe the shock of pleasure it gave
{at last} me to be able to offer to this, the first person I had ever
come across who took less than was given to him, something
{eager} that he was to have. This was a duty that seemed to
me more important than that of a host. Somewhat inconsiderately
I abandoned my guests and ran after him to the garden gate.
"Anton, Anton," {I called down the hill, "I have a coat for you now."
Once more I encountered that serene, tranquil light
in his eyes. He was not in the least surprised that I should
have run after him. Nothing could be more natural to him,
with his philosophy, than that someone who had a coat he did
not need should offer it to {another} someone else who badly wanted
one. He followed me calmly and I hurriedly got the cook to
fetch all my available old clothes, shoes and shirts. He
carefully scrutinised the great pile with his expert eye,

--8--
picked up the coat, tried it on and then said quietly:
"Yes, this will do me." He said this in the tone of a
millionaire's wife choosing a ring at a jeweller's. He
said it not with the air of {one receiving} a present
but in the {restrained} tone of a gentleman who, without asking
the price, decides to take one of the articles brought out
for his inspection in a shop. "Yes, it's very nice," he
said contentedly, then glanced contemplatively at the other
things. "You can give those walking shoes to Fritz in the
Salsergasse, he needs a pair, and the shirts to Josef in the
square, he can patch them for himself. If you like I'll
{uttered} take them along for you." This again he {said} in the
magnanimous tone of one volunteering to do a favour, and I
almost had a feeling that I ought to thank him for distributing
my belongings among people who were complete strangers to me.
Carefully tying up the things into a bundle, he lifted them
on to his shoulder. Then he added in tones of kindly
condescension, "Yes, you're a good fellow. Nice of you to
give all these things away," and vanished.

Strange, no enthusiastic review of any of my books had
ever delighted me so much as {this} naive praise. I have
often thought of Anton, and always with gratitude, for few
people have given me so much spiritual help. Frequently
when I have been worrying about stupid little money matters
I have called to mind this man who lived calmly and confidently
for the day, because he wanted no more than was enough for
that one day. How often have I not seen him in his shabby
clothes, but never otherwise than serene and carefree! And
always I have thought to myself: if everyone in the world
were to learn this secret of mutual trust and confidence,
there would be no police, no courts of law, no prisons and
no money. Our entire complicated economic system, which
reduces itself ever and again to an absurdity and has become

--9--
a "social problem", would perhaps be remedied if everyone
lived like this one man, who only took what he
needed and gave as much of himself as he could. For some
years now I have heard nothing of him. But there are few
people about whom I feel less anxiety: I know that God
will never leave this man in the lurch and - what's more -
men will not, either.

Digest, New York
1939
THE MAN WITH THE SECRET OF HAPPINESS
by Stefan Zweig, 49 Hallam Street
London W1
